terms.definition: Routine clinical measurements indicating basic body function: temperature, pulse, respiration, blood pressure and oxygen saturation.
terms.exampleSentence: Please record her vital signs every 15 minutes until she is stable.
terms.contextNotes: On UK wards routinely abbreviated to 'obs'.

terms.definition: National Early Warning Score 2: standardised NHS bedside score of acute illness severity using six physiological parameters.
terms.exampleSentence: Her NEWS2 has risen from 3 to 7, so I have escalated to the registrar.
terms.contextNotes: Endorsed by RCP; ≥5 triggers urgent review.

terms.definition: Modified Early Warning Score: aggregated bedside score predicting clinical deterioration; superseded by NEWS2 in many NHS trusts.
terms.exampleSentence: Some maternity units still use MEWS rather than NEWS2 for antenatal patients.
terms.contextNotes: MEOWS is the obstetric variant.

terms.definition: A 15-point neurological scale assessing eye, verbal and motor responses to quantify level of consciousness.
terms.exampleSentence: His Glasgow Coma Scale is 13 out of 15, down from 15 on admission.
terms.contextNotes: GCS ≤8 indicates need for airway protection.

terms.definition: Rapid bedside assessment of consciousness: Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive.
terms.exampleSentence: She was alert on admission but is now only responding to pain on the AVPU scale.
terms.contextNotes: Quicker than GCS; used in initial triage.

terms.definition: A pain assessment tool where the patient rates pain intensity from 0 (none) to 10 (worst imaginable).
terms.exampleSentence: She rates her abdominal pain as 8 out of 10 on the numerical rating scale.
terms.contextNotes: Document score before and after analgesia.

terms.definition: A 100mm horizontal line on which the patient marks pain intensity between 'no pain' and 'worst pain'.
terms.exampleSentence: Mark the visual analogue scale at the point that best represents your pain right now.
terms.contextNotes: Useful when language barriers limit verbal scoring.

terms.definition: A pain assessment tool using six cartoon faces from smiling to crying, designed for children and patients with limited literacy.
terms.exampleSentence: Use the Wong-Baker FACES scale to assess his pain — he is only four years old.
terms.contextNotes: Validated for children aged 3 and over.

terms.definition: Localised damage to skin and underlying tissue caused by sustained pressure, usually over a bony prominence.
terms.exampleSentence: On admission she had a category 2 pressure ulcer over the sacrum.
terms.contextNotes: NHS uses category 1-4 plus unstageable and deep tissue injury.

terms.definition: Partial-thickness skin loss involving epidermis and/or dermis, presenting as an abrasion, blister or shallow crater.
terms.exampleSentence: The wound nurse classified it as a category 2 pressure ulcer with a serous-filled blister.
terms.contextNotes: EPUAP/NPUAP categories 1-4 plus unstageable.

terms.definition: An NHS risk-assessment tool estimating an inpatient's risk of developing a pressure ulcer, based on age, BMI, mobility and continence.
terms.exampleSentence: His Waterlow score is 18, so he requires a pressure-relieving mattress.
terms.contextNotes: ≥10 at risk; ≥15 high risk; ≥20 very high risk.

terms.definition: A pressure ulcer risk-assessment tool scoring sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition and friction.
terms.exampleSentence: Her Braden scale score is 12, indicating high risk of skin breakdown.
terms.contextNotes: Lower score = higher risk; ≤12 high risk.

terms.definition: Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool: a 5-step screen identifying adults at risk of malnutrition using BMI, weight loss and acute disease effect.
terms.exampleSentence: Her MUST score is 2, so please refer to the dietitian.
terms.contextNotes: 0 low, 1 medium, ≥2 high risk.

terms.definition: A diagnostic medical aid classifying stool consistency into seven types, from hard lumps (type 1) to entirely liquid (type 7).
terms.exampleSentence: He has been opening his bowels three times today, type 6 on the Bristol Stool Chart.
terms.contextNotes: Types 3-4 considered normal; type 7 suggests diarrhoea.

terms.definition: Structured evaluation of an inpatient's likelihood of falling, considering history, mobility, medications and cognition.
terms.exampleSentence: Please complete a falls risk assessment within six hours of admission.
terms.contextNotes: Use Morse or FRAT depending on trust policy.

terms.definition: The number of breaths a patient takes per minute, counted over a full 60 seconds at rest.
terms.exampleSentence: His respiratory rate is 24, contributing 2 points to his NEWS2.
terms.contextNotes: Normal adult RR is 12-20.

terms.definition: The percentage of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by oxygen, measured non-invasively by pulse oximetry.
terms.exampleSentence: Her oxygen saturation has dropped to 88% on room air.
terms.contextNotes: Target 94-98% (88-92% in chronic CO2 retention).

terms.definition: The pressure exerted by circulating blood on arterial walls, recorded as systolic over diastolic in mmHg.
terms.exampleSentence: His blood pressure is 90 over 50, much lower than this morning.
terms.contextNotes: Always note arm used and patient position.

terms.definition: The time taken for colour to return to a nail bed or skin area after blanching pressure; assesses peripheral perfusion.
terms.exampleSentence: Her capillary refill time is 4 seconds, suggesting poor peripheral perfusion.
terms.contextNotes: Normal ≤2 seconds; central CRT more reliable in adults.

terms.definition: Body temperature measured by an infrared probe placed in the external auditory canal, reflecting tympanic membrane temperature.
terms.exampleSentence: Her tympanic temperature is 38.7°C, please take a paired blood culture.
terms.contextNotes: Less reliable in suspected hypothermia.

terms.definition: A five-level emergency department triage tool assigning urgency from red (immediate) to blue (non-urgent).
terms.exampleSentence: He has been triaged orange under the Manchester Triage System and needs review within 10 minutes.
terms.contextNotes: Used across most NHS emergency departments.

terms.definition: The difference between fluid input (oral, IV, enteral) and output (urine, drains, vomit, stoma) over a defined period.
terms.exampleSentence: Her fluid balance is positive 1.5 litres over 24 hours.
terms.contextNotes: Usually charted hourly post-op or in critical care.

terms.definition: The volume of urine produced over a defined time, used as a marker of renal perfusion and hydration.
terms.exampleSentence: Her urine output has dropped below 0.5 ml/kg/hour for the last three hours.
terms.contextNotes: <0.5 ml/kg/hour for 6h defines AKI stage 1.

terms.definition: A bivalve vaginal speculum used for cervical visualisation during smear taking and gynaecological examination.
terms.exampleSentence: Please warm the Cusco's speculum before insertion to improve patient comfort.
terms.contextNotes: Sims' speculum used for posterior wall inspection.

terms.definition: Listening to internal body sounds, typically with a stethoscope, to assess the heart, lungs and abdomen.
terms.exampleSentence: On auscultation there were coarse crackles at both lung bases.
terms.contextNotes: Document side, location and added sounds.

terms.definition: Examination by touching and feeling the body to assess tenderness, masses, organ size or temperature.
terms.exampleSentence: She has guarding and rebound tenderness on palpation of the right iliac fossa.
terms.contextNotes: Always start with light palpation away from pain.

terms.definition: The reaction of the pupils to light, assessed for size, equality and reactivity as part of neurological observations.
terms.exampleSentence: Pupils are equal and reactive to light, size 3 bilaterally.
terms.contextNotes: PEARL = Pupils Equal And Reactive to Light.

terms.definition: Serial assessment of GCS, pupil response, limb power and vital signs to detect changes in neurological status.
terms.exampleSentence: Please carry out neurological observations every 30 minutes for the first two hours.
terms.contextNotes: Frequency stepped down as the patient stabilises.

terms.definition: Inspection of a surgically created opening for colour, output, peristomal skin condition and bag fit.
terms.exampleSentence: On stoma assessment the spout is pink and well-perfused with formed output.
terms.contextNotes: Dusky or black stoma indicates ischaemia — escalate.

terms.definition: Any aggregated bedside scoring system using physiological parameters to flag deteriorating patients.
terms.exampleSentence: The early warning score is now 6, which mandates a registrar review.
terms.contextNotes: Generic term; specific tools include NEWS2, MEWS, PEWS.
